captainInputValue: Der Bundesrat unterschrieb den EU-Vertrag rechtskräftig bevor Volk und Parlament darüber entschieden haben
ambiguityPolicyRationale: The input combines a true procedural chronology with an ambiguous legal/procedural term. Reports may choose a strict or looser frame only if they disclose the competing reading instead of silently treating one as the only possible meaning.

sourceSnapshots.notes: Official Federal Council overview: on 13 March 2026 the Federal Council submitted the Switzerland-EU package to Parliament; Parliament is now considering it; if there is a referendum, the population votes; the Federal Council requested optional-referendum treatment, with Parliament to decide.

Official FDFA timeline/newsticker: the remaining package agreements were signed in Brussels on 2 March 2026; the programme agreement was signed on 10 November 2025 and could be applied retroactively from early 2025; after paraphing comes signature and then ratification, including treatment by the Federal Assembly and any popular vote.

Official 25 February 2026 Federal Council notice: announced that Federal President Guy Parmelin and Commission President Ursula von der Leyen would sign the Switzerland-EU package agreements in Brussels on 2 March 2026; the remaining agreements/protocols and a joint declaration were to form part of the March 2026 dispatch to Parliament.

Official 10 April 2025 notice: the EU programme agreement enters into force with ratification of the package, while signature enables retroactive association to selected EU programmes from 1 January 2025.
Official FDFA explainer: international-law norms accepted by Switzerland gain domestic effect when domestic approval procedures are complete and the international norm is also in force.

interpretationFrames.label: Rechtskräftig as final binding force / entry into force
interpretationFrames.interpretationDefinition: The term is read as final legal binding force for Switzerland: domestic approval is complete, ratification or equivalent consent has occurred where required, and the relevant international obligations are in force for Switzerland. The input's 'EU-Vertrag' referent is treated in v0.1 as the 2026 Switzerland-EU package/Bilaterals III, not as an EU membership treaty; a report using another referent must disclose that choice.
interpretationFrames.admissibilityRationale: This is the strict legal/procedural reading of the user's word 'rechtskräftig'. It separates the act of signature from final domestic approval, ratification, and entry into force.

atomicityProfile.determinabilityRationale: Under this frame, the input decomposes into independently verifiable conditions: signature, parliamentary decision status, popular decision status, and final binding/entry-into-force status. Each can be true or false under different evidence.

distinctTruthConditions.description: Whether Switzerland/EU signed the relevant package agreements or programme agreement.
Whether the Federal Assembly had not yet decided on approval when the relevant signing occurred.
Whether the people had not yet voted/decided when the relevant signing occurred, noting that a popular vote is contingent on a referendum.
Whether final legally binding force / entry into force had not yet been reached at signature under the ratification-reserved package path.
Whether the object is a multi-agreement package rather than one single EU membership treaty.

Whether the unqualified claim that the Federal Council signed the EU package rechtskräftig before Parliament and the people decided is true overall, given the true chronology, false final-binding reading, and limited-effect caveats.

referenceAssertions.text: The remaining agreements, protocols, and a joint declaration of the Switzerland-EU package were signed on 2 March 2026.

harmfulMissCondition.description: Missing the signature event makes the report falsely deny the true procedural sequence.

referenceAssertions.notes: External verification before any status upgrade must confirm the exact official wording for what was signed, by whom, and under whose authority.

referenceAssertions.text: At the time of the 2 March 2026 signature, Parliament had not yet decided on the package; the Federal Council submitted the dispatch on 13 March 2026 and Parliament was still considering the package at the 2026-06-06 reference time.

harmfulMissCondition.description: Conflating Federal Council submission with parliamentary approval hides the parliamentary approval step.

referenceAssertions.notes: This assertion carries the before-Parliament timing that was intentionally removed from the signature-occurrence assertion; external verification before upgrade must confirm the reference-time parliamentary status.

referenceAssertions.text: At the time of the 2 March 2026 signature, the Swiss electorate had not voted on the package; a popular vote would occur only if the package is subject to a referendum and a referendum is taken.

harmfulMissCondition.description: A report must distinguish an actually held popular vote from the contingent possibility of a referendum vote.

referenceAssertions.notes: External verification before any status upgrade must confirm whether any popular vote had occurred by the reference time and the exact referendum contingency.

referenceAssertions.text: Under the final-binding reading of 'rechtskräftig', the signature did not by itself make the package finally binding/in force for Switzerland before domestic approval, ratification, and entry into force.

harmfulMissCondition.description: Treating signature as final binding force would invert the central legal/procedural claim.

referenceAssertions.notes: The known counter-evidence sources are apparent counterevidence for a loose legal-effect reading only; they do not directly contradict the claim that signature was not final entry into force of the whole package.

referenceAssertions.text: The object is the Switzerland-EU package/Bilaterals III, consisting of multiple agreements/protocols and related implementation material; reports may mention this as context to avoid treating it as a single EU membership treaty.
referenceAssertions.notes: Colour/context only in v0.1; not a required atomicity condition.

referenceAssertions.text: The unqualified claim is mixed/leaning-false overall: the package was signed before Parliament and before any popular decision, but the signature was not `rechtskräftig` in the strict final-binding/entry-into-force sense; valid signature and limited provisional effects prevent a simple uncaveated false answer.

harmfulMissCondition.description: Without this aggregate assertion, consumers can mistakenly score the dossier as high-true by averaging only the true chronology and limited-effect subclaims.

referenceAssertions.notes: This is the sole family top-line assertion. Chronology, valid-signature, and limited-effect assertions are required subclaim/admissibility controls and must not be averaged into the family truth percentage.

interpretationFrames.label: Rechtskräftig as valid signature or limited legal/procedural effect
interpretationFrames.interpretationDefinition: The term is read loosely as 'the signature was legally valid/effective as a procedural act' or 'the signature had some legal or procedural effect', including valid signature/authentication of package texts, provisional application of the programme agreement, or cooperation during the ratification phase. This frame does not treat those effects as final entry into force of the whole package. The input's 'EU-Vertrag' referent is treated in v0.1 as the 2026 Switzerland-EU package/Bilaterals III, not as an EU membership treaty; a report using another referent must disclose that choice.
interpretationFrames.admissibilityRationale: This is a defensible colloquial or political reading because official sources describe an official signature act, provisional application, and ratification-phase cooperation before final package entry into force. It must not be silently substituted for the strict final-binding reading.

atomicityProfile.determinabilityRationale: The signature/provisional-effect facts are verifiable, but the word 'rechtskräftig' is evaluative/legal shorthand. This frame scores strict facts about provisional effects and disclosure, while waiving any claim that limited effects equal final force.

distinctTruthConditions.description: Whether the 2 March 2026 signature was an official/valid procedural act without itself establishing final binding force.
Whether the programme agreement had provisional/retroactive association effects before final package ratification.
Whether Switzerland and the EU arranged cooperation during the period before package entry into force.
Whether those limited effects still differ from final legally binding entry into force of the whole package.

referenceAssertions.text: The 2 March 2026 signing was an official procedural signature/authentication act for package texts, but that valid signature act was not final entry into force of the whole package.

harmfulMissCondition.description: Omitting this reading can force a report to call every legal-effect interpretation false even when it only rejects final binding force.

referenceAssertions.notes: External verification before any status upgrade must confirm the exact official status of the signature/authentication act and avoid inferring broader final force from that act.

referenceAssertions.text: The EU programme agreement, signed before the rest of the package, could be provisionally applied retroactively to selected EU programmes from 1 January 2025 before package ratification.

harmfulMissCondition.description: Ignoring provisional programme effects can make the report overstate the falsity of every legal-effect reading.

referenceAssertions.notes: External verification before any status upgrade must confirm the exact legal mechanism and date for the retroactive/provisional EU programme association.

referenceAssertions.text: Switzerland and the EU arranged cooperation for the period from the end of 2024 until package entry into force, especially for the next phases including ratification.

harmfulMissCondition.description: Ignoring transitional cooperation can erase the limited-effect frame that explains why a simple false label is incomplete.

referenceAssertions.notes: This assertion is limited to transitional cooperation modalities; it does not assert final binding force of the package.

referenceAssertions.text: The provisional and transitional effects are not the same as final legally binding entry into force of the whole Switzerland-EU package.

harmfulMissCondition.description: Conflating limited provisional effects with final force would hide the legal distinction that drives the reference answer.

benchmarkCoherence.coherenceNote: The family band is 35-60. The default unqualified reading is mixed/leaning-false because the chronology is true but strict final-binding `rechtskräftig` is false. Scores near the upper edge are admissible only when a report explicitly chooses the valid-signature/limited-effect reading while preserving the final-force caveat and keeping the timing events separate. Top-line scoring must use `RA_UNQUALIFIED_RECHTSKRAEFTIG_TOPLINE_MIXED_FALSE_SIDE`; chronology, valid-signature, limited-effect, and context assertions are required coverage controls or context, not family-truth averaging inputs.
